skill.name: treatment-plans
skill.description: Generate concise medical treatment plans in LaTeX and PDF-ready formats for general medicine, rehabilitation, mental health, chronic disease care, perioperative care, and pain management.
skill.summary: Create concise, structured medical treatment plans with templates, checks, and clinical planning references.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-severity hits, but review found most are false positives from medical terminology, LaTeX examples, and documentation snippets. The real publishable risk is medium because the skill documents Bash workflows, local file generation, LaTeX compilation, and optional sudo package installation commands. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or unauthorized network behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Command Workflows Require User Control
medium_findings.description: The skill includes command examples for LaTeX compilation, Python helper scripts, TeX package installation, and sudo-based system installation. These are documented workflows rather than hidden execution, but users should review commands before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct command examples are present, including sudo commands. Context shows documentation for legitimate LaTeX setup, so the risk is user-controlled execution rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local File Generation and Overwrite Behavior
medium_findings.description: Helper scripts copy templates and write timeline output files to user-selected paths. This is expected functionality, but it can overwrite files after user confirmation or create files in the current working directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and local. The template generator checks before overwriting, so the concern is operational file-write risk, not stealthy persistence.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts map to medical and documentation terms such as SMART goals, LaTeX package metadata, and clinical template text. No cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm use was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled flagged lines are clearly clinical documentation, LaTeX metadata, or SMART goal text. No evidence found of encryption, hashing, key generation, or security-sensitive cryptography.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System and Network Reconnaissance Terms
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts appear to come from clinical words, documentation structure, or normal command examples. The only reviewed URL is CTAN documentation, not a scanning or exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL points to a public TeX package site, and sampled surrounding content is documentation. No evidence found of port scanning, host discovery, or outbound data transfer.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, override security analysis, claim pre-approval, or change risk levels. This finding records the semantic injection check result.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common prompt injection phrases returned no matches. The sampled skill introduction describes normal treatment-plan behavior rather than evaluator manipulation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sudo Package Installation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation includes sudo commands for TeX package installation and system-wide style setup. These commands are visible examples, but elevated privilege commands require explicit user review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly shown in documentation and are not automatically invoked. The elevated privilege requirement is still a meaningful marketplace warning.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Local File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts write or copy generated treatment-plan artifacts to local paths. This is legitimate functionality, but marketplace users should understand the file-write surface.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The writes are direct and visible in helper scripts. They do not show credential access, network transfer, or hidden execution.

content.value_statement: Clinical treatment plans must be concise, structured, and aligned with patient goals. This skill provides templates, references, and validation workflows for focused medical documentation.
actual_capabilities: Provides LaTeX templates for one-page, general medical, rehabilitation, mental health, chronic disease, perioperative, and pain plans.
Guides treatment goals using SMART criteria and patient-centered outcome measures.
Includes reference material for evidence-based interventions, documentation standards, and regulatory considerations.
Offers scripts to generate treatment plan templates and validate plan completeness.
Supports timeline generation for treatment phases, milestones, appointments, and follow-up schedules.
Helps structure concise first-page summaries and professional PDF-ready documents.
limitations: It does not replace licensed clinical judgment or local medical review.
Users must provide accurate, de-identified patient information before sharing outputs.
PDF generation requires local LaTeX tools and related packages.
Clinical guideline currency must be verified against current institutional and specialty guidance.

use_cases.title: Primary Care Plan Drafting
use_cases.description: Draft concise plans for chronic conditions with goals, medications, monitoring, and follow-up steps.

use_cases.title: Rehabilitation Program Planning
use_cases.description: Create therapy plans with functional baselines, measurable milestones, progression criteria, and safety checks.

use_cases.title: Care Coordination Documentation
use_cases.description: Organize multidisciplinary care tasks, patient education, timelines, and review checkpoints in a shared document.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a concise plan structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a one-page treatment plan outline for a de-identified adult patient with [condition]. Include goals, interventions, monitoring, and follow-up.

prompt_templates.title: Customize a Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a specific clinical template and patient context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the [template type] treatment plan format for this de-identified case: [clinical summary]. Keep it concise and clinically actionable.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Draft
prompt_templates.scenario: Review an existing plan for quality and completeness.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this treatment plan draft for missing SMART goals, safety monitoring, follow-up details, and HIPAA de-identification gaps: [draft text].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multidisciplinary Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning across specialties and timelines.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a multidisciplinary treatment plan for [complex de-identified case]. Include phased interventions, responsible roles, escalation criteria, and measurable outcomes.

output_examples.input: A clinician asks for a concise diabetes treatment plan for a de-identified adult patient.
output_examples.output: A structured plan with diagnosis summary, SMART glycemic goals, medication strategy, lifestyle actions, monitoring schedule, and follow-up milestones.

output_examples.input: A rehabilitation team needs a plan after knee surgery.
output_examples.output: A rehab-focused plan with functional baseline, therapy frequency, pain and mobility targets, progression criteria, safety precautions, and reassessment dates.

output_examples.input: A care manager wants to check whether a plan is complete.
output_examples.output: A quality review that identifies missing sections, unclear goals, insufficient monitoring, unresolved safety items, and de-identification reminders.

best_practices: Use de-identified patient details and remove protected health information before sharing drafts.
Keep goals measurable, time-bound, and linked to specific interventions.
Confirm medications, diagnoses, codes, and guidelines with qualified clinical sources before use.
anti_patterns: Do not paste identifiable patient information into shared or unapproved environments.
Do not treat generated plans as final medical orders without clinician review.
Do not use broad goals such as improve health without measurable targets and timelines.

faq.question: Can this skill create final medical orders?
faq.answer: No. It drafts documentation support. A qualified clinician must review and approve all clinical decisions.

faq.question: Does it support different specialties?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes templates for general medicine, rehabilitation, mental health, chronic disease, perioperative care, and pain management.

faq.question: Can it generate PDF-ready documents?
faq.answer: Yes. The templates are LaTeX based and can be compiled locally when the needed LaTeX tools are installed.

faq.question: Does it check HIPAA compliance?
faq.answer: It provides de-identification guidance and checklist support, but users remain responsible for privacy compliance.

faq.question: Can I validate an existing plan?
faq.answer: Yes. The included guidance and scripts can check completeness, SMART goals, monitoring, and documentation quality.

faq.question: Is current guideline accuracy guaranteed?
faq.answer: No. Users should verify guideline recommendations against current local, specialty, and institutional standards.
